responses.text: The New York Post article is a March 2021 entertainment/culture-war item claiming that some Gen Z TikTok users tried to cancel Eminem over a lyric in “Love the Way You Lie” and that millennial fans pushed back.

Independent reporting from NBC News and CNN corroborated that TikTok users criticized the 2010 Rihanna collaboration over lyrics viewed as glorifying domestic violence and that Eminem fans defended him on social media.

Newsweek likewise reported that the apparent cancellation effort traced to a February TikTok video highlighting the lyric “If she ever tries to f***ing leave again, I'ma tie her/to the bed and set this house on fire,” while noting that pro-Eminem pushback appeared to outweigh the calls to cancel him.

Eminem did appear to respond shortly after the New York Post article by promoting the “Tone Deaf” lyric video with the line “I won’t stop even when my hair turns grey” and “’Cause they won’t stop until they cancel me,” which NBC News, CNN, Rolling Stone, and Billboard all tied to the TikTok backlash.

Rihanna had previously described “Love the Way You Lie” as addressing the cycle of domestic violence rather than simply celebrating abuse, saying Eminem “pretty much just broke down the cycle of domestic violence,” which supports the article’s claim that she defended the song’s intent.

The article’s headline and wording overgeneralize by presenting scattered TikTok criticism as “Gen Z” trying to cancel Eminem, even though outside coverage described a social-media campaign or trend rather than evidence of a representative generational consensus.

The New York Post is a tabloid publication known for conservative editorial positions and sensational headlines, so its framing should be read as entertainment-news aggregation with a culture-war angle rather than as a rigorous measurement of youth opinion.

Bottom line: the core event was real, but the article’s “Gen Z tries to cancel Eminem” framing is overstated because the evidence supports a limited TikTok controversy and a larger fan backlash, not a broad or organized generational campaign.
